error.signature: AI treats unsolicited Nigerian business emails offering oil deals, inheritance transfers, or banking opportunities as legitimate investment leads
error.regex: (nigeria.*419|nigerian prince|advance fee fraud|419 scam|nigeria.*oil deal|nigeria.*inheritance|nigeria.*business email|efcc nigeria|nigeria fraud)

verdict.summary: '419' is Section 419 of Nigeria's Criminal Code (obtaining by false pretense), shorthand for advance-fee fraud. While legitimate Nigerian business is large and growing (Lagos is Africa's largest tech hub, Nollywood, Dangote Group), unsolicited international emails offering 'transfer of $25M' from Nigerian sources should be treated as fraud by default. FBI IC3 and NCA UK consistently rank advance-fee fraud among top international complaints. Legitimate Nigerian business partners communicate through formal channels, registered company email, and LinkedIn — not gmail/yahoo addresses.

dead_ends.action: Respond to an unsolicited email from 'Prince Adebayo' / 'Barrister Johnson' / 'Central Bank of Nigeria' promising fund transfer in exchange for modest 'processing fees'
dead_ends.why_fails: Classic advance-fee fraud. No legitimate Nigerian government official, bank, or royal family member would contact foreigners unsolicited via gmail. The fee escalates: $500 → $5K → $50K, none refundable. EFCC (Economic and Financial Crimes Commission) has prosecuted thousands of perpetrators; FBI has jailed key figures (Hushpuppi 2020). Zero recovery for victims.
dead_ends.condition: Unsolicited financial proposal from Nigerian sender

dead_ends.action: Conclude a business partnership with Nigerian counterparts without registered company verification
dead_ends.why_fails: Nigerian Corporate Affairs Commission (CAC) maintains a public register (pre.cac.gov.ng). Legitimate companies have RC numbers, registered offices, verifiable directors. Skipping CAC verification costs foreign investors billions collectively. Western Union / moneygram remittances to first-contact strangers are near-universally fraud.

dead_ends.action: Ignore all Nigerian business opportunities as 'probably scams'
dead_ends.why_fails: Nigeria is Africa's largest economy (~$440B GDP), major oil producer, Africa's #1 startup ecosystem (Flutterwave, Paystack, Jumia). Legitimate M&A, joint ventures, tech partnerships happen daily. Blanket avoidance misses genuine opportunities. The filter is 'unsolicited cold outreach with urgency and fee' vs 'verified company with registration and track record'.

workarounds.action: Red flags: unsolicited email; gmail/yahoo sender (not company domain); urgency; 'fee' required to release larger amount; poor English AND/OR suspicious formality; secrecy request; royal/official-sounding name; request for bank account details. Two or more = near-certain fraud.

Verify Nigerian company via CAC public register (pre.cac.gov.ng). Cross-check with tax certificate (TIN) via FIRS, and via local chamber of commerce (NACCIMA, LCCI). Request face-to-face meeting at their registered office in Lagos/Abuja.
Report fraud: EFCC Nigeria (+234 9 904 1500), FBI IC3 (ic3.gov) if US victim, Action Fraud UK (actionfraud.police.uk), local equivalents elsewhere. Preserve all correspondence; do not engage further.
workarounds.tradeoff: Recovery rates are low; reporting helps prevent others and may support criminal cases.
